ops.title: P5 live finding: shortlist cost-cohort omission dropped business on the canonical ask (R-I)
ops.body:
The P5 regression capstone's first ask (canonical two-intent business round trip, live, cache-first) caught a doctrine violation the entire offline suite missed: `_cohort_select` cohorted rows only by (date, source) and selected cheapest-mileage-first, so economy filled the 12-slot per-endpoint budget across distinct dates before any business row was reached. All 203 composed journeys and all 6 finalists surfaced economy with phantom cabin misses while `cache query` (zero quota) proved abundant direct Alaska J space both directions (LAX/SAN→HNL and HNL→SFO, 40k mi, 6–9 seats). Present-then-dropped, not a retrieval gap: `_group_best`'s key includes cabin, so the J rows reached the pool and died at selection.

Doctrine "Expansion is budgeted, and truncation is disclosed" requires diversification across date, program, AND cost cohorts — the cost axis was never implemented. Same disease as the July-14 postmortem's origin crowd-out (fixed by the P1 home-origin floor), in cabin form.

**Ruling R-I:** cost cohorts = cabin. The cohort key becomes (date, source, cabin); the round-robin allocates slots to every cabin present. Diversity, not preference: no cabin-preference lookup inside selection (lanes-not-scores — shortlist retrieves, fit/rank judge). Single-cabin pools select byte-identically; the home-origin floor keeps its exact semantics.

Meta-lesson: the offline suite's shortlist fixtures were single-cabin or hand-thinned, so the crowd-out was invisible until a live pool mixed cabins at scale. Selection-diversity changes need a mixed-axis pool fixture per axis (origin, cabin — and the next axis someone adds).
